content: I'm working on a new cataloging system for our rare books collection at the university library. We've got some really unique items - first editions from the 1800s, handwritten manuscripts, even some ancient scrolls. The project is taking longer than expected though.
That sounds fascinating! What makes the cataloging process so time-consuming? Are you using any special preservation techniques?

Well, each item needs individual assessment. I'm wearing cotton gloves and using special lighting to examine the condition. We've got a 15th-century illuminated manuscript that's particularly delicate. I'm also training two new library assistants who started last month.
Cotton gloves and special lighting make sense for preservation. What kind of training are you giving the new assistants? Is it mostly hands-on or do you have formal sessions?

Mostly hands-on, but I created a training manual with protocols. Last week we had an incident where one of them almost used regular tape on a document - could have been disastrous! I'm also preparing for our library's board game night next Thursday. I'm bringing my new favorite, 'Codenames: Deep Undercover Edition.'

Regular tape on a document sounds terrifying! Good thing you caught that. 'Codenames: Deep Undercover Edition' sounds intriguing - is it much different from the original? How many people usually attend these game nights?

It's definitely spicier than the original! Usually we get about 15-20 people. I'm also in charge of snacks - I'm baking my famous sourdough pretzels. Speaking of baking, I tried a new recipe last weekend that didn't rise properly. I think my starter might be off.
Sourdough pretzels sound amazing! What went wrong with your new recipe? And how do you keep your starter healthy? I've always struggled with maintaining mine.

I think I used water that was too hot and killed some of the yeast. I'm going to try again this weekend. Between work and training for my 5K next month, I barely have time for hobbies. I've been doing interval training three times a week - trying to beat my personal best of 28 minutes.
28 minutes is impressive! What's your training schedule like? And do you find that running helps with the stress of work, especially with the new assistants?

I run early mornings, usually around 6am before work. It helps clear my head, especially after those cataloging sessions with delicate materials. I'm also planning a surfing trip to Santa Cruz next month - trying to fit it between the 5K and a conference I'm attending in Portland.
6am runs sound brutal but rewarding! Santa Cruz and Portland back-to-back - you're keeping busy! What's the conference about? Is it library-related or something else?

It's a digital archiving conference - really excited about learning new preservation techniques. I'm also thinking of starting a podcast about rare books and their stories. Maybe call it 'Between the Lines' or something catchy like that. Gotta go though - I have a rock climbing session in 30 minutes and I'm still trying to decide which of my three harnesses to bring!
